segments.s748-g33: به + proper name/epithet + بنمود: a standard dative/benefactive construction 'showed to Shiruy'. بنمود is the past tense (classical/poetic) of 'نمودن' meaning 'showed/displayed'. زان سان is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in that manner / such', and گهر is the direct object 'a jewel'.
segments.s748-g34: A noun phrase headed by a past participle: بریده 'cut off' modifies یکی شاخ 'a single branch/bough'. زرین کمر is a compound adjective 'golden‑girdled' or 'of a golden belt/waist' qualifying the branch; the whole describes the jewel's shape or origin. Word order (participle + numeral + noun + adjectival phrase) is poetic but straightforward: 'a single branch, cut, of a golden girdle.'
segments.s748-g35: چنین گفت is an adverb + verb cluster 'thus said'. The verb گفت is used in the simple past (classical narrative) and the prepositional phrase با باغبان marks the addressee ('to/with the gardener'). Word order here is stylistically flexible: 'Thus Shiruy said to the gardener.'
segments.s748-g36: که‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional ('if' / 'should it be that'). زین here means 'this saddle/trappings' and governs خداوند in a possessive/genitive sense ('the lord/master of this saddle'). گوهر نشان is a nominal phrase 'jewel‑showing' or 'jewel‑bearing' (نشان here characterizes someone as exhibiting or marked by a jewel). Together the clause means 'if the master of this saddle is one who displays jewels.'
segments.s748-g37: نگویی is a prohibitive/negative form (classical usage of ن + present/subjunctive) meaning 'do not say / do not boast'. هم‌اکنون 'right now' is an adverbial. ببرم is first‑person present/subjunctive/volitional from بردن used here in the sense 'I will take/cut off' and together with سرت ('your head') yields the idiom 'I will take your head / I'll behead you.' The clause thus expresses a warning: 'Don't say you'll cut off his head this instant.'
segments.s748-g38: همان را (the very same thing — with را as the definite/object marker) introduces a relative clause: که او باشد از گوهرت 'that he may have from your jewel.' که = 'that/which', او = 'he', باشد = subjunctive/present 'may be / may have' (classical optative sense), از + گوهرت = 'from/of your jewel.' The whole gives the reason: 'the very thing he might claim from your jewel.'
segments.s748-g39: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'). شاها is the vocative 'O king!' and به باغ اندرست is a locative nominal clause meaning 'is in the garden' (اندرست = archaic/poetic present 'is within'). The sequence is the reporter's speech: 'He said to him, "Sire, there is in the garden..."'.
segments.s748-g40: A compact descriptive noun phrase: زره‌پوش (armored / armored warrior) + مردی (a man). کمانی بدست is a compound modifier 'bow-bearing / of the bow, in hand' where کمانی means 'of the bow' (an archer) and بدست literally 'in the hand' (i.e., 'with a bow in his hand'). Together: 'an armored man, an archer with a bow in hand.'
segments.s748-g9: بران is the poetic contraction of بر آن, functioning as a prepositional adverb 'on that' and precedes the noun it governs (شاخ). شاخ here means 'branch' (poetic/archaic word order places the prepositional element before the noun). برمهره is effectively بر مهره 'on (are) beads' with مهره (=bead) used as the noun, زر follows as a postpositive modifier 'gold(en)' and the numeral (پنج) follows the phrase in Classical Persian order: 'five golden beads upon it.'
segments.s748-g10: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the source/class (ز هرگونه مهره = 'from/for every kind of bead'). هرگونه modifies مهره 'bead(s) of every kind.' بسی is an adverb 'much' or 'very' modifying the participial phrase برده رنج; برده is the past participle of بردن (literally 'taken') used with the noun رنج to form the periphrastic expression 'has borne/suffered hardship' (i.e. بسی برده رنج = 'has suffered much'). Word order is somewhat inverted and characteristic of older poetic Persian.
segments.s748-g11: چنین گفت is the fixed classical phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used with a following indirect object); با marks the indirect object 'to/with' and باغبان is the addressee 'the gardener.' شهریار (the king) appears after باغبان by poetic inversion as the subject/agent who speaks. The whole segment thus means 'thus the king said to the gardener.'
segments.s748-g12: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that.' این مهره‌ها is the subject 'these beads.' تا here functions as a purposive/subjunctive marker 'so that / in order that' and combines with آید (subjunctive/present 'may come') in the idiom آید به کار 'to come to use / be useful.' کت is the noun 'coat' and takes the postposition به کار (i.e. 'be useful for the coat'). Overall: 'that these beads may be of use for a coat.'
segments.s748-g13: به بازار شو is an imperative: به + place + شو (imperative of شدن) = 'go to the market.' بهره‌ای is an indefinite object 'a share / some portion.' گوشت خر is a noun phrase in which خر ('donkey') follows and specifies گوشت ('meat of donkey'); Persian allows the modifier after the head noun. So the segment gives an order: 'Go to the market; take a share — some donkey meat.'
segments.s748-g14: دگر نان literally 'other bread' / 'another loaf' (دگر = 'other'). و is the connective 'and.' بی‌راه 'wrong / unwarranted' modifies جایی 'place' (جایی = 'some place / anywhere'), and گذر here denotes passage/passing; together the phrase warns about passing through improper places (poetic, elliptical imperative). Word order (modifier after noun or adverbial phrases) reflects classical style.
segments.s748-g15: مرآن is an object-clitic phrase meaning 'to/for me' (مر = archaic form of me + آن for emphasis). گوهران را is the definite object 'those gems' (را marks the definite object), and بها سی هزار places the noun 'price' (بها) followed by the numeral 'thirty thousand' — classical Persian often puts the quantified noun before or after the numeral.
segments.s748-g16: درم names the coinage 'dirham(s).' بد here is an archaic/poetic imperative form equivalent to بده (give) — a terse command to hand over the money. کسی را is the indirect object 'to someone,' and که بودی به کار is a relative clause 'who would be/will be useful for the task' (بودی is past/subjunctive-form of بودن used in the relative sense; به کار = 'for the work/use'). The segment thus instructs to give the dirhams to a person fit for the job.
segments.s748-g17: سوی + noun expresses motion “toward” (sوی = toward). شد is past of شدن used as a full verb meaning “became / turned” and combines with سبک “light” to give the sense “became light / moved lightly / approached briskly.” باغبان is the subject “the gardener” and follows the predicate in this poetic word order (verb phrase then subject).
segments.s748-g18: بدان here is a compound spatial/pointing phrase (ب- + دان) functioning as “toward/at that” and is followed by شاخ زرین “that golden branch/bough.” ازو is the oblique pronoun “from him/it,” خواست is the simple past “asked/wanted,” and نان is the object “bread / sustenance.” The clause therefore says he asked (from him) for bread regarding/at that golden bough.
segments.s748-g19: بدو + نانوا uses the clitic بدو (“to him”) followed by نانوا “the baker” and then گفت, the past tense verb “said.” کاین is the relative/demonstrative “that/this,” and رابها is an old fused sequence (را + به) functioning as an object/pointing marker or emphasis on the following element; in context it highlights “this (thing).”
segments.s748-g20: ندانم is the negative present/past-clitic phrase “I do not know / I don’t know how.” نیارمت is an archaic/compound negative form meaning roughly “I will not bring / I cannot bring” (sense depends on dialectal use here). کردن is the light verb “to do” (here part of an infinitival or verbal construction), and رها is an imperative/adverbial sense “let go / leave it.” Together the sequence expresses inability or refusal to fetch it and telling the interlocutor to leave it.
segments.s748-g21: ببردند is the past plural “they took / they carried” (ب- prefix + بردند). هر دو “both / the two” is the subject phrase, and به گوهر فروش is the goal “to the gem-/jewel-seller” (به = to, گوهر فروش = gem-seller). Word order is verb → subject → prepositional goal, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s748-g22: که introduces reported speech or purpose “saying / that.” این را has the demonstrative + object marker “this (as object).” بها کن is an imperative-like instructive sequence where بها (value/price) is a noun and کن (do) is the light verb meaning “set a price / make it priced.” بدانش is a dative/clitic sequence meaning roughly “for that / to him/regarding that,” and بکوش is the present imperative “strive / endeavor” — i.e. “do your best to value it.”
segments.s748-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” داننده is an agent noun from دانستن “knower / expert” (here: an expert observer or appraiser). آن مهره‌ها is the demonstrative + plural “those beads/gems,” and رابدید is the past verb ‘saw’ with the object marker ra fused (رَ + دید) — “he saw those beads.”
segments.s748-g24: بدو “to him” + گفت “said” introduces the reported utterance. کاین را is the demonstrative with object marker “this,” followed by که which introduces the content clause. یارد is a noun meaning “companion / beloved / helper” (here a personal name or designation), and خرید is the past of خریدن “bought” (so the clause states that the companion bought it). The sentence thus reports the expert’s identification that the item was purchased by that person.
segments.s748-g57: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. روی شهنشاه is a genitival phrase 'the face of the shah' (noun phrase with noun before possessive title). دید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular of دیدن and governs the preceding object phrase; آن سپاه is the subject (that army/host) placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s748-g58: همه 'all/everyone' is an adverb/quantifier. باز گشتند is a compound verbal idea: باز (back/again) + گشتند (past plural of گشتن 'turn/return') — here 'they turned back/returned'. گریان is a verbal/adjectival participle 'weeping', modifying the subject ('they'). ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' with ز (poetic 'from') + راه 'road'.
segments.s748-g59: یکایک 'one by one' (adverb) sets distributive action. بر here functions as 'toward/to' (they move toward someone). زاد فرخ is a proper name (Zād‑Farrokh) rather than literal 'birth/fortune' — the two words together name a person; hence شدند 'they became' or 'they attached themselves' (شدند, past plural) means 'they came over/entered the service (of) / became followers of' that man. The verb is plural to match the individual members implied.
segments.s748-g60: بسی here is an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage). هر کسی = 'each person' (هر modifying کسی). داستانی is an indefinite noun 'a tale/story'. زدند is the past plural of زدن; in the idiom داستان زدن it means 'to tell (a story)'; among the listed senses the closest is the transitive/performative sense 'to sound/perform'.
segments.s748-g61: که introduces reported speech/explanation: 'that'. ما بندگانیم = 'we are servants' (بندگان + present‑copula‑suffix ‑یم). و = 'and'. او خسروست = 'he is king' (خسرو + copula ‑ست). The present copulas are used to state their humble status and the other's rank.
segments.s748-g62: بدان here is the fused prepositional phrase به + آن = 'for/to that' (classical contraction b + an). شاه 'king' is governed by that prepositional phrase. روز بد = 'an ill/unfavourable day' (روز 'day/time' + بد 'bad'). اکنون نوست is an archaic present construction: اکنون 'now' + نوست = نو است 'is new/has newly come' — together meaning 'has now newly arrived'. So the whole clause means 'for that king an unlucky day has now come.'
segments.s748-g63: This clause expresses a wish/curse. باد here functions as an optative particle 'may (there be/it happen)' followed by the adjective سرد 'cold' to form 'may a cold (wind) ...'. کسی (anyone) is the indefinite subject of the relative/condition expressed by the verbs. برو زد combines two verbs in sequence: برو 'go' (imperative/infinitive sense 'who goes') + زد (past of زدن 'strike/attack') to form the idea 'who goes and strikes/attacks'. نیارد at the start is archaic/poetic and in this context is not the ordinary noun 'friend' — it functions as an interjectional or negative/optative particle in older usage; here it frames the curse (its exact etymology is archaic), so I mark it as an irregular/archaic form.
segments.s748-g64: چه ... چه ... is the correlative construction 'whether ... or ...'. در باغ باشد = 'whether he be in the garden' (در + باغ; باشد is subjunctive/present‑jussive of بودن used after چه). اندر نبرد = 'or in the battle' (اندر = in/within, نبرد = battle). The clause specifies the locations in which the wish/curse applies.
segments.s748-g65: بشد is an archaic past narrative verb meaning 'it happened / he came' (here: 'came'). زاد + فرخ functions as a proper name (Zad‑Farrokh); زاد is here read as the element 'born/origin' forming the name. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه) marking direction/position. Word order is poetic but corresponds to 'X came near the king.'
segments.s748-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source (ز درگاه او = 'from his court/presence'). برد is the past of بردن 'to take/lead off' used narratively ('he took/led off'); چندی سپاه is the direct object 'several/some troops' placed after برد. The whole clause means 'and from his court he took several troops.'
segments.s748-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' نزدیک او رفت is a simple past clause 'he went/approached near him.' تنها ببود uses the archaic past/copular form ببود ('was') with تنها 'alone' — literally 'he was alone.' The sequence gives a temporal frame: 'When he came near him, he was alone.'
segments.s748-g68: فراوان سخن گفت is 'he spoke much / spoke at length' (فراوان modifies سخن; گفت is past 'said/spoke'). The conjunct و links the next clause: خسرو شنود uses شنود, an archaic form meaning 'he heard/listened' (here 'Khosrow listened'). The verb positions and archaic verbal forms are typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s748-g69: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him). اگر ... دهد is a conditional clause 'if the king gives/grants ...' شاه is the subject of دهد. بارم is 'my charge/occasion/burden' (object) — here 'the office/charge I would be given.' The clause functions as the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s748-g70: برین کرده‌ها = 'concerning these deeds' (برین = بر + این, 'on/against/with respect to these'; کرده‌ها = 'deeds/actions'). زینهارم دهد is an optative/request-like clause: زینهار (warning, admonition) with the enclitic ـم (to me) and دهد 'let him give' or 'may he give me a warning.' Together: 'let him warn me about these deeds.'
segments.s748-g71: بیایم is an optative/subjunctive 'let me come' or 'I may come' used to request permission. بگویم is subjunctive 'that I say / let me say' (here expressing intention). سخن هرچ هست = 'whatever speech/words there are' (هرچ = 'whatever'), so the whole clause means 'Let me come and say whatever needs saying.'
segments.s748-g72: وگرنه 'otherwise' introduces the alternative. بپویم is a verbal form meaning 'I will go / I will proceed' (subjunctive/future sense in classical style). به سوی نشست = 'toward the assembly/meeting' (به سوی = toward; نشست = session/assembly). Together: 'Otherwise I shall go to the gathering.'
segments.s748-g25: چنین شاخ is a noun phrase (چنین ‘such/so’ preceding the noun شاخ ‘horn’). در گنج خسرو is a locative phrase ‘in Khosrow’s treasure/treasury’ (در + noun phrase where خسرو marks the possessor). بدی is an archaic/poetic verbal form here linking the clause to an implied second-person subject (‘you put/gave’); its form and function are poetic (past/perfect sense) rather than the noun-sense listed in the lemma table.
segments.s748-g26: برین گونه is an adverbial phrase ‘in this manner/way’ (بر + این/این‌ گونه fused to mean ‘in this kind/way’). هر سال is a temporal phrase ‘each year’. صد نوبدی is a quantifying temporal expression — literally ‘a hundred nūb(d)ī’ — i.e. ‘a hundred times’ or ‘a hundred turns’ per year; نوبدی is an older/poetic form related to ‘نوبت’/‘نوبتی’. The whole segment modifies the preceding clause, giving frequency.
segments.s748-g27: The clause begins with topicalized تو ‘you’, followed by the direct object این گوهران ‘these gems’. از که is the interrogative prepositional phrase ‘from whom’. دزدیده‌ای is the perfect/past-participle-based second-person-singular form ‘you have stolen’ (دزدیده + ای), so the clause reads as a direct question: ‘You, from whom have you stolen these gems?’
segments.s748-g28: گر is a conditional particle ‘if’. از بنده خفته is a prepositional phrase ‘from a sleeping b[N]de/servant’ (بنده ‘servant/slave’, modified postpositively by the adjective خفته ‘asleep’). ببریده‌ای is the second-person singular perfect/past of بریدن ‘to cut/pluck’ (ببریده + ای) meaning ‘you have cut off/plucked (them)’. The clause offers a conditional supposition about how the gems were taken.
segments.s748-g29: سوی is the directional preposition ‘toward’. زاد فرخ here functions as a proper name or compound (Zād‑Farrokh); it is the goal of the motion. شدند is the past plural auxiliary/verb ‘they became/they went/they turned’ and appears before the topicalized subject آن سه مرد ‘those three men’ — a common poetic word order (V‑S).
segments.s748-g30: ابا here is used in the sense of reluctance/refusal (‘to refuse, disdain’), governing the following coordinated objects گوهر و زر ‘gem and gold’. The final phrase با کارکرد ‘with (their) deed/performance’ (با + کارکرد) is an instrumental/adverbial modifier: it indicates accompaniment or that they went with accomplishments/works. Word order places the refusal verbal notion before the objects for emphasis.
segments.s748-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. آن گوهران ‘those gems’ is the subject (poetically personified). زاد فرخ again functions as a proper name (the object seen). بدید is the simple past ‘saw’. The clause uses personification (gems as perceptive) and the normal SOV/P structure of classical Persian poetry is visible.
segments.s748-g32: سوی indicates direction ‘toward’, شهریار نو is ‘the new sovereign/king’ (شهریار + نو). اندر کشید is a compound poetic verb meaning ‘drew/turned into/approached’ — اندر (in/into) + کشید (past of کشیدن ‘to pull/draw’), so the clause means they moved/drew into the presence of the new king. Word order places the directional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s748-g97: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed at the clause start. این زره is a demonstrative + noun phrase ('this armor'). چون is a simile preposition 'like', زمین is its object, and منست is an archaic/polite predicate (third-person) from منس meaning 'is fitting/appropriate' or 'is so'—here functioning like a simple copula/predicate: 'is like the earth.'
segments.s748-g98: سپر آسمان is a genitive/possessive construction 'shield of the sky/heaven.' زرین is a postpositive adjective 'golden' modifying سپر, and the clause closes with the same archaic predicate منست 'is (so)/is fitting.' Word order (noun + modifier + predicate) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s748-g99: دو کوه is the subject 'two mountains.' این دو گنج is a noun phrase 'these two treasures' (demonstrative + numeral + noun). نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed' (classical past), and به باغ is a prepositional phrase 'into/in the garden.' The verb is transitive with the treasures as its object and 'به باغ' marking location.
segments.s748-g100: کزین (کز + این) is an archaic prepositional phrase 'from these.' گنجها is plural 'treasures.' بد here is a stative adjective 'ill/unwell' modifying the possessed noun دلم 'my heart.' م is the clipped poetic form of the copula (from است), attached enclitically to indicate 'is.' چون چراغ is a simile 'like a lamp.' The clause thus means 'from these treasures my heart is ill/aches, like a lamp.'
segments.s748-g101: همانا is an emphatic particle 'indeed/behold.' سرآمد here is used as a noun meaning 'culmination/end' (rather than just 'foremost'), modified by the temporal adverb کنون 'now.' روز من is the genitive 'my day.' The clause is nominal and implies the state 'the end of my day (has) come'—classical Persian often omits an explicit copula/verb.
segments.s748-g102: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' اختر is 'star' and گیتی افروز is a compound where افروز 'illuminating' qualifies the world (گیتی) or the star ('world‑illuminating star'); من at the end is possessive 'my.' Word order places the question word first and the possessor after the phrase, a common poetic arrangement: 'Where is the star that lights my world?'
segments.s748-g103: کجا introduces a question 'where.' آن همه is a demonstrative + quantifier 'all those' and کام و آرام من is the coordinated noun phrase 'my desires and my peace/tranquility' (possession marked by من placed after the coordinated nouns, a poetic order). This is an interrogative nominal phrase asking for the whereabouts of those former states.
segments.s748-g104: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause referring back to 'those desires and my peace.' The clause contains two prepositional phrases introduced by repeated بر: بر تاجها 'on/over the crowns' and بر بدی نام من 'on/over the badness (ill‑repute) of my name.' Classical Persian poetry often omits an explicit verb in such relative clauses, so these locative phrases are understood as the places/conditions where the former joys/respite once were (i.e., now lost). 'بدی نام من' = 'the ill repute/my bad name.'
segments.s748-g73: بدو گفت خسرو چه گفتی بگوی is ordered in a compressed, somewhat inverted way: بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said') + خسرو (the speaker) — i.e. 'Khosrow said to him'. چه گفتی is a direct question/command fragment 'what did you say / what you said', and بگوی is an imperative 'say' (tell it). The phrase strings reported speech into a single line: Khosrow addresses someone and commands them to speak what they said.
segments.s748-g74: نه انده گساری نه پیکارجوی is the reported content — two coordinated negatives connected by نه ... نه. Each phrase is a noun/agent‑phrase describing what the addressee is not: 'not ... and not ...'. The phrases follow a compact, poetic word order; گساری and پیکارجوی are agentive/qualifying nouns ('dissolver/breaker', 'war‑seeker/belligerent').
segments.s748-g75: چنین گفت پس مرد گویا به شاه lines up adverbial/inversional elements: چنین گفت ('thus he said') introduces the utterance; پس ('then') places it in sequence; مرد گویا ('the eloquent man' or 'the man, eloquent/seeming') is the agent; به شاه marks the target 'to the king.' Word order reflects narrative compression: introducer + temporal marker + speaker + addressee.
segments.s748-g76: که در کار هشیارتر کن نگاه is the content clause introduced by که ('that/which'): در کار ('in affairs/matters') is the locative phrase; هشیارتر کن is an imperative construction where هشیارتر ('more alert') is the predicate and کن is the imperative auxiliary 'do/make' (i.e. 'be more alert'); نگاه functions as another imperative/ exhortation 'look/pay attention'. The two imperatives are succinctly juxtaposed — 'be more alert in affairs; attend.'
segments.s748-g77: بر آن نه که is an idiomatic negative framing: 'not on that (basis) that...'. کشتی تو ('your ship') is a metaphor for yourself or your fortune; جنگی هزار places the qualifier after the noun — 'warlike a thousand' or 'warlike a thousandfold' — a compact poetic grouping meaning 'capable of a thousand battles.' The whole segment denies that assumption.
segments.s748-g78: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/ultimately'. سیرآیی here functions as a predicative verbal idea — 'you will become sated/filled' (a future/result sense in classical usage). از کارزار ('of/from battle') completes the idea: 'you will in the end be satiated with battle.' The clause expresses consequence/result after the prior negative assumption.
segments.s748-g79: همه شهر ایران تو را دشمنند: همه شهر ایران ('all the towns/cities of Iran') is the subject phrase. تو را is the object/topic 'you' (marked by را), and دشمنند is the predicate 'are enemies' — note the classical separation of the plural copula into 'ند' after the predicate. The whole sentence states that all Iranian towns are hostile to you.
segments.s748-g80: به پیکار تو یک دل و یک تنند: به پیکار تو ('in fight against you' / 'to fight you') is a prepositional phrase qualifying the relation; یک دل و یک تنند is an idiomatic plural predicate 'they are one heart and one body' meaning 'they are united.' Again تنند shows the predicative + plural suffix pattern; the clause emphasizes unity in opposition.
segments.s748-g113: به سختی is an adverbial phrase meaning “with difficulty / scarcely” that modifies the following predicate. نبودیم is the past negative first-person plural (we/there were not) functioning impersonally here to assert lack of a rescuer. فریادرس is the predicate noun (“rescuer, savior”) after the verb; the order (adverb + verb + predicate) is a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s748-g114: نهان باش is an imperative: نهان (hidden) + باش (be!) — a direct command to conceal oneself. و links two commands. منمای is a classical negative imperative (“do not show/reveal”) followed by its object رویت (your face — رو + possessive ـیت). به کس uses the preposition به + کس (“to anyone/any person”) marking the indirect object; word order here is verb + object + prepositional phrase.
segments.s748-g115: به دستور is a prepositional phrase 'by order / on command' that adverbially modifies فرمود (he said/ordered). فرمود is the past narrative verb used to introduce the command. زان پس ('from that' + پس) functions as 'after that / then', and the proper name قباد (Qobad) appears after the verb as a topicalized subject — a common poetic inversion.
segments.s748-g116: This is a negative imperative clause. کزو is an old pronominal prepositional form meaning 'from him / of him'; هیچ + مکن gives a strong prohibition ('do not at all / never do'). بر + بد expresses doing something 'against / upon' someone (i.e. harm). نیز is an adverb 'also / even', and یاد here is a noun 'remembrance / mention' (i.e. 'do not even mention him'). The sequence follows classical word order where the negation and verb govern the clause.
segments.s748-g117: بگو is an imperative 'say / tell'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / let them'), here marking what should happen as a result of the order. سوی + place is the directional phrase 'toward', and طیسفونش is 'Ctesiphon' with the possessive/third-person suffix -ش used here as an enclitic referring to the person to be taken. برند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form of بردن ('let them carry / take'), commonly used after بگو + تا to give an instruction.
segments.s748-g118: بدان here is an archaic contraction functioning like به آن 'to that' (preposition + demonstrative) and introduces the destination 'that house'. خانه is the noun 'house'. رهنمونش is a noun 'guide / direction' with the possessive suffix -ش ('his guide' or 'guide him') functioning as the object to be brought. برند again is the 3rd‑person plural verb 'let them bring/lead'. The clause therefore orders that they bring/guide him into that house.
segments.s748-g119: بباشد is a jussive/optative form meaning 'may it be / let it be' (a wish or benediction). به آرام ما روز چند is a prepositional/temporal phrase: به آرام = 'in/with peace', ما indicating 'for us/our', روز = 'days', and چند = 'a few / some' — together 'may our days be peaceful for some days.' The order (predicate + possessor + noun + quantifier) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s748-g120: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / there must not'. که‌دارد is the relative clause 'that has' (که + دارد) — i.e. 'who has'. کس is an indefinite subject 'anyone / someone'. او را گزند is the object phrase 'harm to him' (او را = him with the object marker را; گزند = harm). The whole clause means 'There should be no one who has power to harm him.' Word order follows classical Persian: indefinite subject + object phrase + verb in relative clause.
segments.s748-g105: ببردند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they brought/took' (word order: verb before object is common in poetic prose). پیلی is an elephant (poetic adjective/noun meaning 'elephantine/elephant'). به نزدیک is a small prepositional phrase 'to/toward near' marking direction/position, and اوی is the pronoun 'him/it' referring back to the person mentioned in context.
segments.s748-g106: پر از درد شد is a light-verb construction: پر از درد 'full of pain' (پر + از) followed by شد 'became' (past tense). جان تاریک اوی places the affected noun 'جان' (soul, life) modified by the adjective 'تاریک' (dark) and the possessive/pronominal suffix اوی 'his'; overall: 'his dark soul became full of pain.'
segments.s748-g107: بر آن کوهه پیل بنشست شاه is inverted but literal: بر 'on/upon' + آن 'that' + کوهه 'mount/mountain' + پیل 'of the elephant' (a poetic compound meaning the elephant's back/seat), then بنشست (past tense 'sat down') and شاه 'the king' as subject; so 'the king sat on that elephant-mount.'
segments.s748-g108: ز باغش بیاورد لشکر به راه is a clause with the preposition ز 'from' indicating origin (باغش 'his garden'), بیاورد 'he brought' (present/past narrative sense 'brought'), and لشکر 'army' as object; به راه 'onto the road' marks direction. The sequence is source → action → object → direction.
segments.s748-g109: چنین گفت زان پیل بر پهلوی is 'thus said (he) to that elephant at (his) side.' چنین 'thus/so' modifies the speech act; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (word-order places the verb before the rest in poetic style); زان 'that' points to the elephant (پیل) and بر پهلوی 'at the flank/side' gives the spatial relation.
segments.s748-g110: که‌ای گنج اگر دشمن خسروی is the beginning of direct address and a conditional: the standalone particle/combination 'که‌ای' functions here like a vocative 'ای' attached to introduce 'O ...' (address), گنج 'treasure' is the vocative noun, then اگر 'if' introduces the condition and دشمن خسروی 'enemy of the sovereign/kingly' completes it. The clause is left unfinished and continues in the next line.
segments.s748-g111: مکن دوستی نیز با دشمنم is an imperative negative: مکن 'do not' (prohibitive), followed by دوستی 'friendship' (object; idiomatically 'befriend' or 'make friendship'), نیز 'also/even', با 'with', and دشمنم 'my enemy' (possessive). The order reflects a prohibition: 'Do not also be friends with my enemy.'
segments.s748-g112: که امروز در دست آهرمنم is a causal/explanatory clause introduced by که 'for/because.' امروز 'today/nowadays' sets time; در دست here is an idiom meaning 'in the power/control of' (در + دست), and آهرمنم 'Ahriman' names the demonic power—the pronominal suffix (م) marks the speaker's relation ('I am in Ahriman's hand').
segments.s748-g49: This prepositional phrase z + place marks the origin of motion: ز (from) + بازار (market). نان‌آور is a compound noun 'bread‑bringer' (one who brings/supplies bread) and is coordinated with نان خورش ('bread and stew / his food') by و. The whole phrase functions adverbially to modify the following verbal phrase (it answers 'from where / from what place').
segments.s748-g50: هم‌اکنون is an adverb 'just now / immediately' placed before the verb. برفتم is the past 1st‑person singular of the verb 'to depart/go away' (classical برفتن → 'I went/left'); note the lemma senses supplied do not match this verbal use so its sense is marked separately. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind'; از برش is a short locative/ablative phrase 'from beside him' (از = from; برش is a bound form meaning 'his side / from him'). The whole clause gives the main action: 'I immediately departed, like the wind, from his side.'
segments.s748-g51: بدانست is a past tense verb 'knew / perceived' (verb first, as is common in classical Persian narrative). شیروی functions as a descriptive name/epithet 'the lion‑like / brave one' and is the agent of the verb. کو is the interrogative/relativizing 'where' embedded in a relative clause (i.e. 'knew where ...'), and خسروست is 'the king is' or 'where the king is' (خسرو + copula‑like suffix). Word order here is verb — subject (epithet) — embedded interrogative phrase.
segments.s748-g52: که introduces a subordinate clause giving a reason or explanatory comment ('for / that'). دیدار means 'meeting / seeing', او 'him', در زمانه 'in the age / at that time', and نوست is the archaic adjectival/copular form 'is new'. Together this clause explains or comments on the previous clause: 'for a meeting with him in that age is new/remarkable.'
segments.s748-g53: This is another classical VSO ordering: ز درگاه (from the court) gives the place of departure, رفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/left', and سیصد سوار is the subject 'three hundred horsemen' appearing after the verb for narrative emphasis. The preposition ز governs the place noun, and the numeral + noun is the sentential subject.
segments.s748-g54: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'; باد is the comparison term 'the wind'. دمان (دامان) here is a poetic image 'skirt/hem' often used of garments or capes and in cavalry contexts evokes flowing cloaks or the flanks of horses — it participates in the simile describing the swift motion. تا لب جویبار is a directional limit 'as far as the bank of the stream' (تا = up to/as far as; لب = brink/bank; جویبار = stream). The whole clause modifies رفتند, describing how and how far they rode.
segments.s748-g55: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the subject 'the king' (a proper name used as sovereign), ز دور 'from afar' modifies the seeing‑action, آن سپه را is the definite object 'that army' with را marking the direct object, and بدید is the past tense 'saw'. The clause is a prototypical temporal subordinate ('when the king saw that host from a distance').
segments.s748-g56: به پژمرد is an inchoative/result phrase: به + verb/state ('he became / he paled / he withered') — an idiomatic way to mark a sudden change of state. و connects the two coordinated actions. شمشیر کین is a genitive compound 'sword of vengeance' (شمشیر = sword; کین = vengeance), and برکشید is the past 'he drew/raised' — here specifically the sense 'to draw (a weapon)'. The sequence describes his immediate bodily reaction and then the drawing of the sword.
segments.s748-g1: همی‌بود is a poetic imperfect/past-continuous marker meaning 'was/continued to be' and comes before the subject (خسرو). بران is a compressed prepositional demonstrative meaning 'on/at that' and modifies مرغزار (meadow). The whole phrase reads literally 'was Khosrow on that meadow' = 'Khosrow was there in that meadow.'
segments.s748-g2: This is a nominal phrase describing the tree: درخت (tree) + adjective بلند (tall). ازبرش is a contracted, poetic genitive/locative phrase meaning 'from its side/with its branches' (here indicating the tree's foliage), and سایه‌دار is an adjectival compound 'shade-bearing' = 'shady.' Word order is noun + modifiers; the phrase qualifies the setting under which Khosrow stood.
segments.s748-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بگذشت is the past tense verb 'passed' (time passed). نیمی ز روز دراز is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'half of the long day' (نیمی = half; ز = poetic 'of/from'; روز دراز = long day). Together: 'when half of the long day passed.'
segments.s748-g4: This clause describes the arrival: بنان (literally 'fingertips') is used idiomatically for a beseeching or respectful posture; آمد is simple past 'came.' آن پادشا is 'that/the king' (an emphatic article + ruler), and رانیاز means 'a petitioner/supplication' (here 'a suppliant'). Together: 'a suppliant came to the king (in a beseeching posture).' The phrase is somewhat idiomatic in the poem.
segments.s748-g5: به باغ اندرون بد یکی پایکار is a locative clause: به باغ اندرون 'into the inner garden.' بد (literally 'bad'/'ill') qualifies یکی پایکار ('a certain challenger/contestant'), so the sense is 'a certain ill/ill-omened challenger entered the inner garden.' یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain/someone.'
segments.s748-g6: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' نشناختی is a past-tense verb meaning 'did not recognize' (classical past form), and چهره شهریار = 'the face of the king.' Together: 'who did not recognize the king's face.'
segments.s748-g7: پرستنده راگفت is the poetic object-marker را attached to پرستنده + گفت 'said/was said to'; the ordering here is inverted. خورشید is the agent 'the sun.' فش is a short noun meaning 'press' or 'pressing' and here functions in a compressed, idiomatic way introducing the speech; overall the clause is best understood as 'the sun said to the worshipper...' (i.e., the sun addressed the worshipper).
segments.s748-g8: که introduces the content of the speech 'that/that which.' شاخی (adjective 'branchy' or 'having a branch') qualifies the following noun; گهر is 'a gem/jewel.' زین کمر is a genitival phrase 'from/of that girdle/sash' (زین = this/that harness/girdle, کمر = waist/girdle), and بازکش here is an imperative 'draw out/pluck' (poetic imperative). The line thus records the sun's command: 'pluck a jewel from that branch's girdle.'
segments.s748-g41: This is a compact series of similes. 'ببالا' (preverbal adjective/adverb) gives a lofty/raised quality; classical Persian often places such descriptive words before the nouns they qualify. 'چو' introduces the simile 'as/like', followed by the simile-term 'سرو' (cypress). The second clause repeats the pattern with 'به رخ' ('with respect to countenance') + 'چون' + 'بهار' to mean 'with a face like spring.' Note the absence of an ezāfe; classical/poetic Persian often omits the connective and allows juxtaposition to express modification.
segments.s748-g42: 'بهر' here works as a prepositional/adverbial marker meaning 'with regard to / in respect of'; it governs the following noun 'چیز' ('thing/everything'). 'ماننده' is a postpositive comparative 'like/similar to' (classical form often placed after the noun it modifies), and 'شهریار' is the standard noun 'king' serving as the reference of the comparison. Together: 'in every respect like a king.'
segments.s748-g43: 'سراسر' ('throughout') plus 'همه' ('whole/entire') form an emphatic prepositional phrase modifying 'باغ' ('garden'). The short poetic sequence 'زو' is a contraction of 'از او' ('from him / because of him')—in the printed word-list it is split as 'ز' and 'و' but functionally here it marks the agent/source for the brightness; the predicate 'روشنست' (a fused adjective + copula) appears at the end as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s748-g44: The clause begins with the comparative marker 'چو' ('as/like') followed by 'خورشید' ('the sun') and the participial adjective 'تابنده' ('shining'). 'در' is the locative 'in', and 'جوشنست' is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'is a cuirass / is cuirassed' (جوشن + است), so the whole simile reads 'like the shining sun in a cuirass' (a vivid, armored radiance).
segments.s748-g45: 'فروهشته' is a perfect/passive-participle-like form meaning 'scattered/fell down.' It is followed by the source 'از' ('from') and the noun phrase 'شاخ زرین سپر' ('the golden branch/horn of the shield'). Classical poetic Persian often omits the ezāfe, so the modifiers appear in sequence instead of with -ِ. The whole segment describes something (jewels) having been scattered from that part of the shield.
segments.s748-g46: 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral emphasis). 'بنده' is 'servant/slave'. The prepositional phrase 'در پیش او' ('in his presence' / 'before him') places the servant spatially, and 'با کمر' literally 'with (a) waist' is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'girt about the waist' or 'wearing a belt' (کمر = girdle/belt); the verb 'stood' is implied by the narrative sequence (typical in epic poetry: descriptive nominal phrases follow in sequence).
segments.s748-g47: 'برید' is an old past-tense verb (from بردن) meaning 'he took/cut off' here indicating removal. 'این‌چنین' ('such/thus') modifies what was taken, 'شاخ گوهر' is a noun phrase 'a branch/horn of jewels' (again no ezāfe in the poetic register), and 'ازوی' is the contracted 'از وی' ('from him'), placed at the end to mark the source/possessor. Word order is typical Classical Persian: verb earlier then the descriptive noun phrase and then the source clitic.
segments.s748-g48: 'مراداد' here is a proper name (Muradad) rather than a verbal noun. The coordinating 'و' links clauses, and 'گفتا' is the archaic past 'he said' (گفت + archaic -ا). 'کز' introduces what he said ('that/that from which'), 'ایدر' is an archaic form of 'here', and 'بپوی' is an imperative/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'seek/pursue!' with the prefixed b- typical of classical Persian forms; together it gives a short command reported by the narrator: 'From here, pursue!'
segments.s748-g121: برو is an imperative 'go'. The phrase that follows expresses the action to be carried out: بر + موکل کنند استوار is an object/goal phrase meaning 'to make/appoint (someone) entrusted and steadfast'. کنند is the verbal element (present/subjunctive/imperative-coupled sense 'make/do') used here in an injunction sense linked to برو; استوار is an adjectival complement describing the state to be effected.
segments.s748-g122: گلینوش را is the definite direct object (را marks Glinush as the one being appointed). با introduces the accompaniment/instrument 'with', سواری هزار is a genitive/quantified phrase 'a thousand mounted men / cavalry' (مرکب numeral + noun). The whole segment supplies the patient and the resources of the appointment.
segments.s748-g123: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. گردنده گردون is a compound noun phrase with the participial adjective گردنده 'revolving' modifying گردون 'the sky/heaven'. به سر بر بگشت is a verbal phrase 'returned to its head/origin' (به = to, سر = head/start, بر بگشت = returned); the verb is in past tense. The whole clause gives the temporal setting.
segments.s748-g124: شد is the past copula/verb 'became' or 'it happened that'. آن شاه را سال marks 'that king's year' with را as a definite/accusative marker; the following بر سی و هشت gives the amount/number 'to thirty and eight' (سی و هشت). In idiomatic Persian this yields 'the king's years became thirty‑eight' (i.e., his age reached 38).
segments.s748-g125: کجا here functions as an interrogative/relativizing adverb 'where/when/how'. ماه آذر is 'the month Azar' (month name). بدی is ambiguous in form here (it does not match the provided lexical senses) and seems to function verbally or as a connector in the original line; روز دی names the day/month Dey. Overall this segment sets a calendrical/time reference (month Azar turning into Dey).
segments.s748-g126: گه is an adverbial element 'on the occasion / then'. The remainder is a nominal list: آتش 'fire', و connects items, مرغ بریان 'roasted bird/poultry' (بریان is the adjective following the noun), و connects again, می 'wine'. The clause is verbless (typical in Persian poetry): the existence/abundance of these items is implied.
segments.s748-g127: قباد is the subject (a proper name). آمد is the simple past 'came'. و links the clauses. تاج بر سر نهاد is a transitive clause 'placed the crown upon (his) head' (تاج = crown, بر سر = on the head, نهاد = placed/put — past of نهادن).
segments.s748-g128: به آرام is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in calm/quiet fashion'. بر تخت marks location 'upon the throne'. بنشست is the past verb 'sat down' or 'took his seat'. شاد is a predicative/adjectival complement 'happy/joyful' describing the subject. Word order is typical: adverbial phrase + location + verb + adjectival comment.
segments.s748-g81: بپا is an imperative/interjection meaning ‘stand up’ or ‘wait’. تا introduces the following clause with a purposive/expectational sense (‘so as to / in order to see’), followed by the interrogative چه ‘what’. خواهد is the future auxiliary ‘will’ and is followed by the verbal infinitive نمودن; this is a periphrastic future ‘will do/show’. سپهر (heavens/sky) is the semantic subject of نمودن even though it follows the verbal complex (typical verb-final or flexible word order in classical Persian).
segments.s748-g82: مگر here is a modal particle working like ‘perhaps/might’ (introducing an alternative hope); کینها is the plural abstract noun ‘hatreds/feuds’. بازگردد is a third-person singular verb in a subjunctive/optative sense ‘may return / might turn back’. به مهر is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into affection (love)’, where به marks direction or change of state.
segments.s748-g83: بدو is the prepositional phrase ‘to him’ (بِ + ضمیر) indicating the indirect object; گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (here reported speech). خسرو is the speaker’s name (subject). که introduces the content clause. آری is an agreeing particle (‘indeed/yes’) and رواست is روا + است: روا ‘fitting/permissible’ plus the copula 'is' (stressed predicate).
segments.s748-g84: همه بیمم is a nominal phrase: همه ‘all/entire’ + بیم + enclitic -م ‘my’ = ‘all my fear’ (i.e. my whole fear). از مردم is a prepositional phrase ‘from/of the people’. ناسزاست is a fused predicate (ناسزا + است) meaning ‘is an insult/abuse’ — the whole sentence states the source/content of his fear.
segments.s748-g85: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that’/‘that they might’). پیش من is a locative phrase ‘before me / in my presence’. آیند is third-person plural present/subjunctive ‘they come’ (used here with potential meaning). و links clauses; خواری is the noun ‘humiliation’ and کنند is the plural present/subjunctive ‘they do’ (i.e. ‘they humiliate’). The object of خواری کردن is implicit (me), given the preceding پیش من.
segments.s748-g86: برم بر is an idiomatic cluster: برم here contains the pronominal clitic -م ‘to/for me’ attached to a verbal/prepositional stem indicating something befalling or being upon ‘me’, and the following بر reinforces the idea ‘upon/on’. مگر introduces the exception/alternative ‘unless’. کامگاری is an abstract noun ‘success/fulfillment’ and کنند is the plural subjunctive ‘they do/bring about’. Grammatically, the clause means ‘it will be upon me unless they bring success’ (i.e. unless they succeed).
segments.s748-g87: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is the past tense ‘he heard’. از marks the topic/source ‘about/of’. زاد is the noun ‘birth/origin’ and here heads the genitive phrase 'birth of Farrokh' (زَادِ فرخ). فرخ is a name/epithet attached to زاد; سخن ‘talk/news’ completes the object: ‘he heard talk/news of the birth of Farrokh’. Word order puts the verb before the following object phrase, common in narrative Persian.
segments.s748-g88: دلش is the possessive phrase ‘his heart’ (دل + enclitic -ش). بد شد is the compound verb ‘became bad/ill’ — idiomatically ‘was distressed’ or ‘grew troubled’. از روزگار کهن is an ablative phrase ‘because of / by the ancient course of fate’ (از + روزگار ‘times/fate’ + کهن ‘old/ancient’), giving the cause or tenor of his distress.
segments.s748-g89: 'که' introduces this subordinate clause ('that'). 'او را' is the direct object with the enclitic object marker را (object = 'him'). 'ستاره شمر گفته بود' is a reported-name construction: گفته بود is the past perfect auxiliary (had called/said), so the whole segment means 'they had called him "Shamr's star".' Word order is V (گفته) + auxiliary (بود) with the object marked before the predicate.
segments.s748-g90: 'ز گفتار ایشان' is a causal/agentless prepositional phrase ('because of/from their speech'); ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'. 'برآشفته' is a predicative adjective 'agitated/disturbed' and بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause reads 'he was agitated by their speech.' The phrase modifies the subject introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s748-g91: 'که' again introduces a wish or clause ('that/that it be'). 'مرگ' is the noun 'death' and 'توباشد' is the fused old/poetic form meaning 'may it be to you' / 'may (it) be' (subjunctive/wish). 'میان دو کوه' is the locative phrase 'between two mountains'; here the verb expresses a curse/wish about the locus of the death.
segments.s748-g92: 'بدست' expresses agency/instrument ('by/at the hand of'). 'یکی بنده' is 'one/a certain servant' (یکی + noun), 'دور از گروه' is a postpositional phrase 'far from the group/comrades'. Together they specify the agent and circumstances: 'at the hand of a single servant, far from his company.' Word order places the agent phrase after the locative.
segments.s748-g93: This is a parallel, nominative description: each phrase begins with یکی ('one') + کوه ('mountain') and a following adjective (زرین 'golden' / سیم 'silver'). The elliptical copula is understood (i.e. 'one mountain [is] golden, one mountain [is] silver'). The repeated یکی emphasizes two distinct mountains.
segments.s748-g94: نشسته is a participial/past adjective 'seated' or 'sitting' and functions here as the main predicate; 'تو' (you) is the subject following the verb (typical poetic V‑S order). 'اندر میان دل' is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the heart/mind' and 'به بیم' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in fear' or 'with anxiety.' The whole segment says 'you sit in the middle (of your) heart, in fear.'
segments.s748-g95: 'ز بر آسمان تو' is a poetic ordering meaning 'as for the sky above you' or 'above you, the sky'. ز here is a poetic preposition ('from/with regard to'), بر is the locative 'on/above', and تو marks the possessor 'your'. 'زرین بود' is the predicate 'was golden' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s748-g96: This sequence lists three predicates: 'زمین آهنین' (the earth iron: 'the earth was iron/steely') followed by 'بخت پرکین بود' where بخت is 'fate/fortune' and پرکین is a proper name; بود is the past copula. Grammatically, 'بخت پرکین بود' reads 'fortune was Perkin' or more idiomatically 'your fortune belonged to Perkin.'
segments.s748-g129: ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = 'from' in a poetic register). بر + کرد form a verbal complex: کرد is the past-tense main verb 'did/made' and the preceding بر is a poetic/preverbal particle that helps form the action; the object of کرد is بیعت سپاه ('the army's pledge' = 'the army's allegiance'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetry, but the verb (کرد) comes finally and takes 'بیعت سپاه' as its object.
segments.s748-g130: درم is the direct object 'dirhams / coin (pay)'; داد is the past verb 'gave'. یک ساله ('one‑year') quantifies the payment — 'a year's (worth of) dirhams'. از گنج شاه is a prepositional source phrase 'from the king's treasure' (از = 'from/of', گنج = 'treasure/treasury', شاه = 'the king').
segments.s748-g131: نبد is an archaic/past negative form (classical نبُد) meaning 'there was not / did not last'. پادشاهیش is 'his kingship' (پادشاه + possessive suffix -ش). جز is the preposition 'except' and هفت ماه is the time expression 'seven months'. Together: 'His kingship was nothing except seven months.'
segments.s748-g132: This line is elliptical and addressed to the addressee (تو = 'you'). خواهیش here is the stem خواه ('to want/desire') with the third‑person possessive -یش, yielding 'his desire/wish'. ناچیز is an adjective 'insignificant'. خوان in classical Persian can be a verbal root related to 'call/read' but that sense is not listed in the provided senses; here it functions as a verb ('call/consider'). خواه at the end is a hortative/optative 'wish/imperative' ('if you will / desire'), addressed to the king (شاه). A natural reading: 'You — call his desire insignificant, if you will, O king.'
segments.s748-g133: چنین است is a fixed phrase 'thus it is' or 'so it is'. رسم is 'custom' or 'practice'. سرای جفا is a genitive construction where سرای (here 'house/abode') is followed by جفا ('cruelty / injustice') — 'the house/abode of cruelty' — so رسم سرای جفا = 'the custom/practice of that place of cruelty.' Note: سرای here is a noun 'house/abode' (the lemma provided in the data refers to a different root, but in this line it functions as 'place/house').
segments.s748-g134: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / it should not be'. کزو = 'from which / from it' (ک+ ز + او). چشم داری is an idiomatic combination: چشم ('eye / look') + داشتن ('to have') = 'to expect / to hope for' (literally 'to have an eye (for)'); داری is the present 2sg form 'you have' but with نباید the clause is general/implied. وفا is 'fidelity / faithfulness'. Together: 'One must not, from it, have the expectation of faithfulness' → 'do not expect faithfulness from it.'

sentences.s748-s17: He showed Shiruy such a jewel — a single cut branch of a golden girdle.

sentences.s748-s6: Thus the king said to the gardener that these beads should come in useful for a coat.
sentences.s748-s7: Go to the market and take a share — some donkey meat; get other bread, and do not pass through any wrong place.

sentences.s748-s32: May a cold wind strike anyone who goes and strikes — whether he be in the garden or in battle.

sentences.s748-s34: When he came near him he was alone; he spoke at length, and Khosrow listened.

sentences.s748-s36: Let me come and say whatever there is to say; otherwise I will go toward the assembly.
sentences.s748-s13: Such a horn you placed in Khosrow’s treasure; in this fashion a hundred times each year.
sentences.s748-s14: You — these gems, from whom did you steal them? If you plucked them from a sleeping servant.
sentences.s748-s15: Those three men turned toward Zād‑Farrokh, declining jewel and gold, and with (their) deeds/performance.
sentences.s748-s16: When those gems saw Zād‑Farrokh, they drew toward the new sovereign.
sentences.s748-s49: Now this armor is like the earth; the shield of heaven is golden.
sentences.s748-s50: Two mountains have placed these two treasures in the garden; from these treasures my heart is ill, like a lamp.

sentences.s748-s52: Where are all those desires and my peace — which were upon crowns, upon the ill repute of my name?

sentences.s748-s38: Thus the man then spoke to the king: 'Be more alert in matters — pay heed.'
sentences.s748-s39: Not on the presumption that your ship is battle‑worthy a thousand times; in the end you will grow sated of warfare.
sentences.s748-s40: All the towns of Iran are enemies to you; against you in battle they are of one heart and one body.

sentences.s748-s59: Tell them to take him to Ctesiphon and to lead him to that house.
sentences.s748-s60: May our days be tranquil for a while; no one should have the power to harm him.
